skill.name: go-playwright
skill.description: Expert capability for robust, stealthy, and efficient browser automation using Playwright Go.
skill.summary: Enable Claude to write production-grade browser automation scripts using the Playwright Go library with best practices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill provides legitimate browser automation using Playwright Go. All static findings are false positives: markdown code examples flagged as shell commands, documentation URLs flagged as network risks, and session/cookie management misinterpreted as credential access. No malicious intent detected. The skill is appropriate for web testing and automation use cases.

content.user_title: Write browser automation with Playwright Go
content.value_statement: This skill enables Claude to write production-grade browser automation scripts using Playwright Go with proper architecture, logging, and human-emulation patterns for testing and web scraping.
actual_capabilities: Implement context-based architecture for efficient browser management
Add structured logging with Uber Zap for production observability
Create human-like interaction patterns to avoid bot detection
Handle session management with cookie save/load functionality
Debug automation scripts with headless mode and slow-motion options
limitations: Requires Playwright drivers and browsers installed in the environment
Cannot solve CAPTCHAs - only detects and reports them
Highly sophisticated anti-bot systems may still detect automation
Full browser instances require significant RAM and CPU resources

use_cases.title: Automated web testing
use_cases.description: Write end-to-end tests for web applications using real browsers to verify complex user interactions and dynamic content.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers testing web applications

use_cases.title: Web scraping with anti-bot bypass
use_cases.description: Extract data from websites that use Cloudflare or similar protection by implementing human-emulation techniques.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers and developers building data pipelines

use_cases.title: Browser-based workflow automation
use_cases.description: Automate repetitive browser tasks like form submissions, data entry, or report generation from web dashboards.
use_cases.target_user: Operations teams automating manual browser workflows

prompt_templates.title: Basic browser automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Go script using Playwright to open a browser, navigate to [URL], and print the page title.

prompt_templates.title: Login and session management
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Playwright Go script that logs into [URL] with credentials, saves the session cookies to a file, and loads them on subsequent runs.

prompt_templates.title: Human-like form filling
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a script that fills out a form on [URL] with human-like typing delays, random mouse movements, and viewport randomization to avoid detection.

prompt_templates.title: E2E test with logging
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete Playwright Go test suite for [website] that includes Zap structured logging, proper error handling with panic recovery, and context isolation for each test.

output_examples.input: Write a script to scrape product titles from an e-commerce site
output_examples.output: A Go script that launches Chromium, navigates to the product page, extracts titles using CSS selectors, and logs results with Zap JSON logging.

output_examples.input: Automate login and extract dashboard data
output_examples.output: A script that creates an isolated browser context, performs login with human-type input, extracts data from the authenticated dashboard, and handles errors gracefully.

best_practices: Always use browser contexts over launching new browser instances for better performance and isolation
Implement proper defer statements to close pages, contexts, and browsers to prevent resource leaks
Use Zap structured logging instead of fmt.Println for production debugging and traceability
anti_patterns: Avoid using Fill() for input - use Type() with random delays to simulate human typing
Do not use default timeouts - always set explicit timeouts for actions
Avoid launching a new browser for each task - reuse the browser instance with new contexts

faq.question: What is Playwright Go used for?
faq.answer: Playwright Go is a Go binding for the Playwright browser automation library. It enables programmatic control of Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit browsers for testing, scraping, and automation tasks.

faq.question: Does this skill require installing browsers?
faq.answer: Yes. You need to run 'go run github.com/playwright-community/playwright-go/cmd/playwright@latest install --with-deps' to install the required browser binaries.

faq.question: Can this skill bypass Cloudflare protection?
faq.answer: The skill includes human-emulation techniques that can bypass basic Cloudflare checks. However, extremely strict configurations or paid Cloudflare plans may still detect automation.

faq.question: How does context-based architecture improve performance?
faq.answer: Browser contexts are lightweight and can be created in milliseconds, while launching a new browser takes seconds. Reusing a single browser with multiple contexts provides isolation without the overhead of multiple processes.

faq.question: Can I use this for web scraping?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill is designed for both testing and web scraping. The human-emulation features help avoid detection when scraping sites with anti-bot protection.

faq.question: What logging library does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill uses Uber Zap (go.uber.org/zap) for structured JSON logging in production and human-readable logging in development mode.
